coredata.dc:title: A novel ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of jasmonic acid in crops
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Yongqiang
Kong, Dandan
Li, Wenting
Wei, Yuzhen
Wei, Xiao
Qu, Fangfang
Zhang, Yahui
Nie, Pengcheng
Feng, Xuping
He, Yong
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe was proposed for sensitive detection of jasmonic acid (JA) based on NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs. The prepared NCQDs, with uniquely dual-emissive performance, are insensitive to JA due to electrostatic repulsion. Interestingly, the introduction of Co-MOFs not only avoided the self-aggregation of NCQDs, but changed the surface charge of NCQDs and triggered the response of NCQDs to JA. More importantly, the imprinted recognition sites from MIPs provided "key-lock" structures to specifically capture JA molecules, greatly improving the selectivity of the probe to JA. Under the synergistic actions of Co-MOFs and MIPs, JA can interact with NCQDs through photo-induced electron transfer (PET), resulting in the changes on emission intensity of the probe at Em = 367 nm and 442 nm. Based on the observations, the quantification of JA was realized in the range of 1–800 ng/mL with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.35 ng/mL. In addition, the probe was used for detecting JA in rice with satisfactory analysis results, indicating the probe holds great potential for monitoring JA levels in crops. Overall, this strategy provides new insights into the construction of practical probes for sensitive detection of plant hormones in crops.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Jasmonic acid
Ratiometric probe
Fluorescence detection
Nitrogen doped carbon dots
Co-MOFs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)
Preparation of molecular imprinting fluorescent probe
JA levels determination for real sample
Characterization of morphology and chemical structures
Determination of JA levels in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation of (a) NCQDs, (c) Co-MOFs and (e) NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs; (b) The TEM image of NCQDs and the SEM images of (d) Co-MOFs and (f) NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) FTIR spectra of the prepared materials; (c) The full XPS spectrum of NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs; The high resolution XPS spectrum for (d) C 1s, (e) N 1s and (f) Co 2p.

(a) Dependence of emission intensities of NCQDs on excitation wavelength and (b) the corresponding contour map; The emission intensities of (c) NCQDs and (e) NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs with or without the presence of JA. (d) Measured Zeta potentials of the prepared materials; (f) Spectral overlap between UV–Vis absorption spectra of JA and fluorescence spectrum of NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs.

Fluorescence spectra of the probe exposed to different concentrations of JA (0–800 ng/mL) under the excitation of (a) 320 nm and (b) 378 nm; (c) The dependence of response values on JA concentrations; (d) Response values of the proposed probe to several potential interferences with 100 ng/mL of JA and 500 ng/mL of other interferents; (e) The fluorescence outputs of the probe exposed to 100 ng/mL of JA with 4 h of continuous measurements; (f) Dependences of the performance of the probe for JA detection on storage time of 0–30 days.
A schematic diagram of the proposed probe for the quantitative detection of JA in crops.
Jasmonic acid concentration in rice leaves determined by the fluorescence and HPLC-MS methods.

head.ce:title: A novel ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of jasmonic acid in crops

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe was proposed for sensitive detection of jasmonic acid (JA) based on NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs. The prepared NCQDs, with uniquely dual-emissive performance, are insensitive to JA due to electrostatic repulsion. Interestingly, the introduction of Co-MOFs not only avoided the self-aggregation of NCQDs, but changed the surface charge of NCQDs and triggered the response of NCQDs to JA. More importantly, the imprinted recognition sites from MIPs provided "key-lock" structures to specifically capture JA molecules, greatly improving the selectivity of the probe to JA. Under the synergistic actions of Co-MOFs and MIPs, JA can interact with NCQDs through photo-induced electron transfer (PET), resulting in the changes on emission intensity of the probe at Em = 367 nm and 442 nm. Based on the observations, the quantification of JA was realized in the range of 1–800 ng/mL with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.35 ng/mL. In addition, the probe was used for detecting JA in rice with satisfactory analysis results, indicating the probe holds great potential for monitoring JA levels in crops. Overall, this strategy provides new insights into the construction of practical probes for sensitive detection of plant hormones in crops.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Jasmonic acid (JA), as a major phytohormone, plays important roles in the regulation of crop growth, development and defense against various biotic and abiotic stress involving fungal and bacterial infections [1]. However, the exact mechanism of action for these effects remains elusive and requires further exploration. The sensitive determination of JA is thus essential to unveil its regulatory mechanisms in crops. Traditional methods have been reported for the detection of JA including Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay (ELISA) [2,3], Gas Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (GC-MS) [4], High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) [5], Capillary Electrophoresis-Laser-Induced Fluorescence (CE-LIF) [6]. However, these methods usually require expensive instruments and skilled technicians, and they are also labor-intensive and time-consuming. These deficiencies make them unable to meet the rapid development of crop phenotype research. With this background, several emerging technologies have been proposed for phytohormone determination, such as electrochemical [7,8], surface enhanced raman spectroscopy (SERS) [9], fluorescence [10,11] and localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) [12]. Among these methods, fluorescence assay is attracting increasing attention of researchers due to its favorable features of high sensitivity, fast response and simple operation [13,14].

Several studies have been reported on employing fluorescent probes for detection of phytohormones and biomarkers in crops such as ethylene [15,16], salicylic acid [10,17], 1-naphthalene acetic acid [18] and indole-3-butyric acid [19]. However, there have been still relatively limited reports on the application of fluorescence method for the detection of crop hormones, especially for JA. Besides, most of the fluorescent probes developed for biomarkers monitoring in crops could only emit single-photon emission, which may suffer from interference from surrounding environment to a certain extent [20]. In contrast, ratiometric fluorescent probes with dual-emissive properties have self-regulating function that could mitigate the above mentioned issues. Besides, conventional ratiometric probes are usually constructed from two luminescent quantum dots (QDs) or organic dyes, whose preparations are inevitably accompanied by complex synthesis. Fortunately, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), among many fluorescent materials, have excellent photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield, high photostability and tunable emission peaks [21,22]. Especially, studies show that the CQDs synthesized by chemical regulation could have dual-emission or even multi-emission properties [23]. CQDs have thus potential to replace bimaterial systems for constructing ratiometric probes, but the stability and specificity of unfunctionalized CQDs are still in the pending.

Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) are crystalline materials with three-dimensional networks consisting of organic linkers coordinated to metal nodes. Due to their huge pore volumes, tunable architectures and controllable surface functionalities, MOFs have been widely applied in catalysis, separation, energy transfer and sensing [24]. Previous studies showed that introduction of quantum dots in the synthesis of MOFs could prevent self-quenching by aggregation of quantum dots, improving the stability of sensing systems [25]. On the other hand, molecular imprinting is a biomimetic technology that could produce a polymer with molecular recognition capabilities specific for its target molecule [26]. In addition to their high affinity binding to receptors, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) possess the advantages of great stability, facile preparation and low cost, and they have been considered as desirable candidates to replace biological receptors such as enzymes, antibodies and aptamers [27]. Therefore, developing a CQDs-based ratiometric probes, combining the properties of MOFs and MIPs, may be a good strategy for JA detection.

Herein, a novel ratiometric probe was proposed to detect JA based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots@cobalt-based metal-organic frameworks@molecularly imprinted polymers (NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs). As depicted in Scheme 1 , the NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs with dual-emissive properties were prepared via a facile hydrothermal method. Besides, Co-MOFs and MIPs served synergistic roles in capturing JA molecules, which improved the sensitivity and selectivity of the probe toward JA. Upon the addition of JA into NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs solution, JA could interact with NCQDs through photo-induced electron transfer (PET), resulting in the changes of fluorescence signals of the probe. As far as we know, there have been few studies on the detection of jasmonic acid utilizing fluorescence analysis coupled with the collaboration between MOFs and MIPs. Moreover, the fascinating dual-emissive characteristic of NCQDs and the changed properties of NCQDs by MOFs are also new and interesting discovery. With this strategy, a ratiometric fluorescent probe was rationally designed and prepared for the sensitive detection of JA, and the working mechanism of the probe was investigated and discussed in detail in this work. This study provides new insights for the development of low-cost and highly sensitive methods for the detection of plant hormones.
The reagents and apparatuses were listed in the document of “Supplementary material”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: NCQDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method [28]. Specifically, 0.5 mmol of o-Phenylenediamine (OPD) and 0.5 mmol of citric acid were mixed in 10 mL of dimethylformamide (DMF) solution and followed by stirring for 10 min, and then the mixture was transferred to a reaction kettle to react for 24 h at 180 °C to obtain the NCQDs sample. Next, the sample was centrifuged for 10 min at 9000 rpm, after which the supernatant was retained and dialyzed against distilled water for 48 h using dialysis membrane to remove impurities. Finally, the dialyzed NCQDs were preserved at 4 °C for further use.

Co-MOFs was prepared by a facile method as described previously [29]. Briefly, 400 mg of MelM and 200 mg of Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (II) were dissolved in 10 mL of methanol, respectively. Then they were mixed together quickly followed by stirring for 2 h, and the product was aged at room temperature for 24 h without stirring. Next, the raw product was centrifuged for 15 min at 10 000 rpm, and the precipitates from the above solution were collected and washed twice with ethanol and distilled water to obtain Co-MOFs. Finally, the purified Co-MOFs was dried in vacuum for 12 h at 60 °C and stored at 4 °C until use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of molecular imprinting fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The molecular imprinting (MIPs) fluorescent probe was prepared on the surface of Co-MOFs nanomaterials by sol–gel method. Firstly, 3 mL of Co-MOFs solution (100 mg/mL) and 2.6 mL of NCQDs was mixed under magnetic stirring for 20 min. Then, 10 μL of APTES and 100 mg of JA were added into the above mixture with stirring for 2 h. After that, 10 μL of ammonium hydroxide and 500 μL of TEOS were added followed by stirring for 12 h at dark ambient. Subsequently, the obtained products were centrifuged for 20 min at 9000 rpm to remove excess reagents, and the precipitates were collected and eluted with methanol to remove JA template. Finally, the resulting products were dispersed in 10 mL of distilled water to obtain NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs probes. Besides, the non-imprinted probe without JA (NCQDs@Co-MOFs@NIPs) was also synthesized under the same conditions to check the performance of the probes.

500 μL of the prepared NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs solutions were incubated with 500 μL of JA standard solutions at different concentrations for 18 min. Then the fluorescence spectra were measured with excitation of 320 nm and 378 nm, respectively, and the emission intensities were recorded at 367 nm and 442 nm, respectively. Finally, using the ratio of the fluorescence intensities at the above peaks to establish standard curve for quantitative detection of JA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: JA levels determination for real sample
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, three rice varieties were selected as research objects, one of which was resistant to bacterial blight (Nanjing 46), and the other two were susceptible to bacterial blight (Yongyou 9 and Jiaheyou 218). Samples of three kinds of rice were collected from Zhuji Experimental Field of Zhejiang Academy of Agricultural Sciences. After collection, samples were pretreated according to previous literature [25]. After this, they were used to determine the content of JA in themselves by fluorescence method as described in part 2.2.4 and High-performance Liquid Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (HPLC-MS) method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of morphology and chemical structures
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, TEM and SEM were employed to characterize the surface morphology of prepared materials. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the NCQDs with dual-emissive property was synthesized utilizing citric acid and OPD as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. The TEM of NCQDs shows they have a round shape with suitable dispersion (Fig. 1 (b)), and the inset reveals their lattice spacing is 0.32 nm, which was consistent with the (002) planes spacing of graphitic carbon [30]. Then as depicted in Fig. 1 (c), Co-MOFs was prepared using Co2+ and MeIM as dopants and cross-linkers, and their morphology structure was shown in Fig. 1 (d), it can be found that Co-MOFs is dodecahedral with clear boundary contour and smooth surface. This is similar to the results reported in other work [31], indicating the successful preparation of Co-MOFs. At last, Co-MOFs was employed as core materials, NCQDs as the signal reporter, JA as the template molecule and APTES and TEOS as the functional monomers and cross linker to prepare the NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs probe after removing the embedded template JA (Fig. 1 (e)). Fig. 1 (f) shows the SEM image of the synthesized NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs. The enlarged detail in the inset from Fig. 1 (f) reveals that NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs has a spherical shape and possesses ambiguous boundary contour and rough surface, which is obviously different from Co-MOFs. Besides, in order to demonstrate the elution of JA template molecules, the nanomaterials obtained after each washing were characterized by absorption spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S1, with the increase in elution times, the absorbance of the material at 204 nm gradually decreased, and after five times of elution, that reached to the minimum which was close to the background signal intensity, indicating that JA template molecules were successfully eluted.

EDS was used to characterize the composition and distribution of elements in NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs nanomaterials (Fig. S2 (a)). Fig. S2 (b) shows the sample consists of five elements such as carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, silicon and cobalt. The distributions of these elements in the surface of the sample were shown in Fig.S2 (c-f). It can be found that nitrogen from NCQDs, silicon from MIP and cobalt from Co-MOFs are mainly distributed on the NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs material. Moreover, in order to prove that the molecularly imprinted layer was synthesized on the surface of Co-MOFs, the exposed cross section of the material was characterized by the line scan mode of EDS spectrum. The results showed that the signal of cobalt from Co-MOFs was strong in the middle of NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs while the EDS signal of silicon from MIPs was distributed at both ends (Fig. S3), suggesting the NCQDs@Co-MOFs modified with MIPs were successfully prepared in this work.

XRD measurement was conducted to analyze the crystallographic structure of prepared materials. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), NCQDs@Co-MOFs presented distinct diffraction peaks located at 2θ = 7.2°, 10.3°, 12.6° and 17.9°, which are indexed to (011), (002), (112) and (222) crystal facets of Co-MOFs [32]. Besides, the broad diffraction peak at 23.5° for NCQDs@Co-MOFs can be attributed to CQDs and also indicates the low degree of crystallinity of CQDs. NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs showed similar diffraction peaks to those observed in NCQDs@Co-MOFs pattern. Notably, the intensities of diffraction peaks at 2θ from 5 to 18° belonging to Co-MOFs decreased while that of the broad peak at 2θ from 19 to 30° was increased. This result implies that MIPs were modified to the surface of Co-MOFs. The chemical structures of the materials were confirmed by FT-IR spectra and the results were shown in Fig. 2 (b). NCQDs exhibited three characteristic absorption bands at 1383 cm−1, 1635 cm−1 and 3454 cm−1, corresponding to C–N, CO and N–H stretching vibration, respectively. Interestingly, the C–N stretching vibration peak of NCQDs@Co-MOFs was blue-shifted from 1383 cm−1 to 1302 cm−1, which can be ascribed to the electronic interactions between NCQDs and Co-MOFs [30]. NCQDs@Co-MOFs showed the characteristic band of Co–N stretching vibration at 423 cm−1, and the absorption bands appearing in the range of 500–1580 cm−1 were associated with imidazole stretching and bending modes in 2-methyl imidazole [31], indicating 2-methyl imidazole acted as the major ligand in NCQDs@Co-MOFs. Significantly, NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs possessed main characteristic peaks of Co-MOFs and NCQDs but the corresponding peak intensities decreased, revealing the molecularly imprinted layer was modified on the surface of Co-MOFs.

To further explore the surface elemental composition and chemical structures of NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs, X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was performed and the recorded spectrum was depicted in Fig. 2 (c). The major peaks observed at 153, 285, 399, 531 and 781 eV were associated with Si 2s, C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and Co 2p, respectively. It is noteworthy that silicon can also be found in XPS results of NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs, similar to the EDS results from SEM, confirming the formation of molecularly imprinted layer in the prepared composites. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s shows peaks at binding energies of 284.1, 284.7, 286.4 and 288.2 eV related to CC, C–C, C–N/C–O and CO/CN bonds, respectively (Fig. 2 (d)) [25], confirming the existence of oxygen/nitrogen functional groups in the NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs composites [33]. The spectrum of N 1s shown in Fig. 2 (e) can be deconvoluted into three distinct peaks at 398.8, 400.1 and 400.7 eV related to C–N, CN and N–H groups, respectively, which were assigned to pyrrolic nitrogen and nitrogen in heterocyclic ring [34], indicating NCQDs were successfully integrated into Co-MOFs. The spectrum of Co 2p shows the two characteristic peaks at binding energies of 780.5 and 796.4 eV, which were assigned to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 2 (f)), confirming the presence of Co2+ from Co-MOFs. These results show that the NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs composites was successfully synthesized in this study.

The excitation dependence of emission intensity for NCQDs was explored and recorded as shown in Fig. 3 (a), and the corresponding contour map was illustrated in Fig. 3 (b). With the increase in excitation wavelengths from 300 to 350 nm, the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs gradually increased, and the maximum peak intensity was observed at the emission wavelength of 367 nm when excited at 320 nm (Fig. 3 (b)). With further increases in the excitation wavelengths from 350 to 450 nm, the second fluorescence peak of NCQDs appeared, and a maximum emission peak localized at 442 nm was noticeable at an excitation wavelength of 378 nm (Fig. 3 (b)). Interestingly, the fluorescence peak at the emission wavelength of 367 nm does not change with the increase in excitation wavelength, while the second peak at the emission wavelength of 442 nm red-shifted with the increase in that (Fig. 3 (a)). Previous studies have shown that the degree of pyrrolic nitrogen helps the formation of the conjugated π system, which will lead to a red shift in the emission intensity of CQDs [35]. Therefore, in this study, the fluorescence peak (Em = 442 nm) with the excitation wavelength of 378 nm can be ascribed to the fluorophore of pyrrolic nitrogen while the peak (Em = 367 nm) belonged to the fluorophore monomer of aminobenzene.

The response properties of NCQDs and NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs to jasmonic acid (JA) were further explored in this work. As shown in Fig.3 (c) and Fig. S4 (a), when a certain amount of JA was added to the NCQDs solution, the emission intensities of NCQDs at 367 and 442 nm were almost unchanged. To explain the above-mentioned experimental phenomena, the zeta potentials of prepared materials were investigated and the results were displayed in Fig. 3 (d). It can be found NCQDs and JA showed similar negative charges. This indicated that there were electrostatic repulsion between NCQDs and JA, which makes it difficult to interact for each other. As a result, JA has little effect on the fluorescence performance of NCQDs. On the other hand, NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs had opposite electropositive properties, which provides convenience for its interaction with JA. Besides, the non-imprinted probe synthesized without JA template molecules (NCQDs@Co-MOFs@NIPs) was also used to test their response to JA. As shown in Fig. S4 (b) and (c), with exposure to the same amount of JA, the NCQDs@Co-MOFs@NIPs displayed a certain degree of response to JA. EDS results showed that the zeta potential of NCQDs@Co-MOFs@NIPs was 12.4 eV, significantly lower than that of NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs, implying the presence of electrostatic interactions between NCQDs@Co-MOFs@NIPs and JA in spite of this interaction is much weaker than that of NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs.

A different phenomenon was observed when the same amount of JA was added to the NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs solution. As shown in Fig.3 (e) and Fig. S4 (b), the emission intensity of NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs at 367 nm was decreased while that of the probe at 442 nm was notably enhanced, indicating the proposed probe has better sensing performance for JA compared with NCQDs and NCQDs@Co-MOFs@NIPs. This may be attributed to the synergistic effect of Co-MOFs and MIPs in capturing JA molecules. Besides, fluorescence quenching of a probe can be generally interpreted by Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET), Inner Filter Effect (IFE) and Photo-induced Electron Transfer (PET) theories [36]. For the occurrence of FRET and IFE behaviors, the emission spectra of the fluorescent donor must be markedly overlapped with the UV absorption spectra of the acceptor [25]. Therefore, the spectral overlap of the probe and JA was further characterized as shown in Fig. 3 (f). The results showed that there was little spectral overlap between them, which excluded the possibility of FRET and IFE behaviors. To verify the possible mechanism, fluorescence lifetime of the probe was measured before and after the addition of JA. As shown in Fig. S5 (a) and (b), the fluorescence lifetime of NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs varied from 2.27 ns to 1.74 ns (T1) when exposed to JA, indicating there is a dynamic quenching process in the system and further revealing that the excited NCQDs interacted with JA by energy or electron transfer according to dynamic quenching theory [37]. However, the characterization of UV absorption spectra negated the possibility of energy transfer. This indicated that the fluorescence quenching of the probe was caused by PET behavior, which may be due to the fact that JA can interact with NCQDs through the charge transfer between carboxyl group of JA and amino group of NCQDs under the temptation of molecular imprinting layer and electrostatic attraction. Therefore, as a result, the emission intensity of the probe at 367 nm was reduced and that at 442 nm was increased due to the PET behavior. Based on the above phenomena and analysis, the quantitative detection of JA may be realized using the NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs probe in this work.

Some potential factors that affect the performance of the prepared probe were optimized, including NCQDs amount, pH and incubation time. Firstly, keeping all the other synthesis parameters unchanged, the effect of NCQDs amount on the fluorescence property of NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs was investigated. As shown in Fig. S6 (a), with the increase in dosage of NCQDs, the fluorescence intensity of the probe was increased gradually and then that reached the maximum when the amount of NCQDs was 2.6 mL, but the emission performance of the probe cannot be improved more when further increasing the dosage of NCQDs. This may be due to the spillover effect caused by excessive NCQDs, and the excess parts would not be modified on the probe, but were removed with multiple washing. Then, the effect of pH changes on the fluorescence performance of the probe was investigated, and 100 ng/mL of JA was prepared and employed as the tested subject before the evaluation began. The response signals of the probe to the target analyte at different pH were recorded as shown in Fig. S6 (b). It can be found the output signal increased dramatically with increasing pH value from 4.5 to 6.0 and reached the maximum at pH 6.8 and then it decreased as the pH changed from 6.8 to 8.4. This result showed that weak acidic or neutral pH environment was more conducive to the performance improvement of the probe for JA detection, which may be due to the fact that the active amino group of NCQDs would be inhibited in the strong acid environment while the alkaline environment caused JA to undergo denaturation and resulted in a decrease in detection performance of the probe. Therefore, the optimal pH value for the detection system was pH 6.8. Fig. S6 (c) displayed the ratio of fluorescence signal changes over incubation time. The response value gradually increased with the prolong of the co-incubation time and tended to plateau up to 18 min, indicating an incubation time of 18 min was sufficient for finishing the detection of JA.

The sensing performance of the prepared probe at different concentrations of JA was determined under the optimal experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), with the increase in JA concentration from 0 to 800 ng/mL, the emission intensity of the NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs probe at 367 nm gradually decreased. On the other hand, the fluorescence signal at 442 nm was obviously enhanced (Fig. 4 (b)). These observations can be attributed to the fact that the specific imprinted sites related to JA molecules on the surface of NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs firstly captured JA in solution through electrostatic attraction, and then the JA interacted with NCQDs inside the probe by electron transfer, resulting in the changes of fluorescence intensity of the probe. Fig. 4 (c) described the correlation between JA concentrations and response values of the probe, and two linear regression equations can be obtained as follows: Y = 0.0904X+0.4089 (R2 = 0.994) and Y = 0.0034X+0.4960 (R2 = 0.992), and they were employed for evaluating JA levels over the concentration range of 1–10 ng/mL and 10–800 ng/mL, respectively. Based on this data, the limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were calculated to be 0.35 ng/mL and 1.04 ng/mL according to the principle of Signal-to-noise ratio (S/N = 3.0 for LOD and S/N = 10.0 for LOQ), respectively. Moreover, Table S1 listed the analytical performance of this strategy with other reported methods for JA detection, and the comparison results showed that the performance of the probe was comparable with that of the reported methods as for the detection range and determination limit. Significantly, in comparison to other detection techniques, the proposed approach offers several advantages such as relatively low cost, easy availability, simple implementation and short analysis time.

Selectivity is critical factor to evaluate the potential of practical application of a probe. In this case, 500 ng/mL of other potential interferents (Na+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Cl−, NO3 −, Salicylic Acid (SA), Indoleacetic Acid (IAA), Gibberellic Acid (GA), Abscisic Acid (ABA) and Methyl Jasmonate (MeJA)) and 100 ng/mL of JA were employed for checking the specificity of the probe to JA. As shown in Fig. 4 (d), it suggested that the probe had little or low response to most of these interferents, even if the concentration of the disruptors was 5 times higher than that of JA. This may be benefited from the molecular imprinting strategy and the high sensitivity of the prepared NCQDs to JA. Notably, MeJA can influence the response of the probe to a certain extent. This may be because MeJA has a structure similar to JA, which successfully deceived the “trust” of the molecularly imprinted layer in the probe. However, due to the functional similarities between the two, the response of the probe to MeJA will not greatly affect the detection results.

The short-term and long-term stability of the probe was further explored. Short term stability will allow the operators to have enough time to prepare samples before fluorescence detection. As shown in Fig. 4 (e), after 4 h of continuous measurements, the fluorescence signal of the system remained stable and had no significant difference from the initial output, indicating the probe has good short-term stability. After this, the proposed probe was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for 30 days, and then the fluorescence response of the probe, exposed to 100 ng/mL of JA, was measured and recorded every five days. The results showed that the response signals of the probe decreased gradually with the prolongation of storage time and reached equilibrium in about 20 days, and the final response value was reduced by 5.1% compared with the initial value (Fig. 4 (f)), which may be caused by the partial oxidation of the active groups of NCQDs during storage. Overall, the long-term stability of the probe was within acceptable limits for practical application. Nevertheless, there are still some deficiencies and limitations in this study. For example, the interesting luminescence mechanism of NCQDs at different excitation wavelengths was not deeply explored, and the used Co-MOFs have potential heavy metal pollution problems, which may be a better strategy to use other green MOFs materials to replace Co ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of JA levels in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: This work verified the practicality and reliability of the prepared probe by detecting the JA content in the leaves of rice resistant (Nanjing 46) and infected varieties (Yongyou 9 and Jiaheyou 218). As shown in Table 1 , JA content in the leaves of disease resistant rice (Nanjing 46) detected by the probe and HPLC method was 169.6 ng/g and 175.3 ng/g, respectively, while JA content in the leaves of infected varieties using fluorescence and HPLC method was 74.1 and 71.9 ng/g for Yongyou 9, 48.5 and 52.4 ng/g for Jiaheyou 218, respectively. The relative standard deviation (RSD, N = 6) of fluorescence method is less than 5.14%. The paired t-test was used to evaluate whether there was significant difference between the two methods. The results showed that the P value was>0.05, indicating that there was no significant difference between the two methods. Data analysis shows that the jasmonic acid content of resistant rice varieties is higher than that of infected rice varieties, indicating that resistant rice varieties can increase the level of jasmonic acid content through gene expression, thereby enhancing the stress resistance of rice plants, and ensuring that diseases cannot affect plant growth and development. The above results show that the developed probe has sufficient robustness and reliability, and can be used in practical applications.

In this work, a new ratiometric fluorescent probe based on NCQDs@Co-MOFs@MIPs was developed for JA detection. The prepared NCQDs, with superior dual-emissive performance, hardly interacted with JA due to electrostatic repulsion, resulting in low fluorescence responsiveness to JA. Co-MOFs and MIPs was subtly introduced into the system to solve the sensitivity and specificity of the probe. Co-MOFs can not only increase the contact area between NCQDs and substrate, but also change the electrical properties of NCQDs, making them easier to interact with substrates. The imprinted recognition sites from MIPs prevented the interaction for NCQDs with other interference molecules. JA can interact with NCQDs through the charge transfer under the temptation of molecular imprinting layer and electrostatic attraction, and the emission intensity of the probe at 367 nm was reduced while that at 442 nm was increased due to the PET behavior. With this strategy, the probe exhibited excellent performance for the quantification of JA within the range of 1–800 ng/mL with the LOD of 0.35 ng/mL. The satisfactory analysis results in rice leave samples indicated the great potential of this method for detecting JA in real samples with the merits of high sensitivity and stability, low cost, simple implementation and short analysis time.

Yongqiang Shi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Dandan Kong: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Wenting Li: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Yuzhen Wei: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Xiao Wei: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Fangfang Qu: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Yahui Zhang: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Pengcheng Nie: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Xuping Feng: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yong He: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32071895), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (K20210123).
